structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: This preface section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document provides an overview and essential information for understanding its content. It introduces key concepts such as the design model, target configuration models, and the benefits of using a model-based configuration approach. The document outlines various modeling strategies and defines important elements like parameters and references, emphasizing their roles in both the definition and value sides. Additionally, it details the change history and intellectual property rights associated with the work, ensuring users are aware of its intended use and limitations.

structure.title: 1 Introduction
structure.summary: This section introduces the M2-level configuration model for the AUTOSAR adaptive platform, focusing on its role in creating target-configuration content. It defines key concepts such as target-configuration and design-level models, emphasizing that the M2 model serves as a structural foundation for individual functional clusters. While no specific tables are mentioned, it outlines the relationship between the M1 (target-configuration) and higher-level design models, highlighting how the M2 model facilitates the detailed configuration process.

nodes.title: 1.1 Document Conventions
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document outlines the conventions used in its technical writing. It introduces key elements such as the use of mono-spaced fonts for technical terms and a specific format for presenting constraints, which are crucial for ensuring consistency with AUTOSAR standards. The document also describes class tables that detail attributes of classes within the UML model, providing clear definitions and usage contexts. Importantly, it specifies when certain constraints should be enforced during the development workflow to prevent misconfiguration, highlighting the binding times associated with different model categories.

structure.title: 2 Conceptual Aspects
structure.summary: This section of "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" delves into the foundational conceptual aspects necessary for understanding the adaptive machine configuration process. It introduces key terms such as "adaptive platform," which refers to a system capable of dynamically adjusting its resources and configurations based on real-time needs, and "machine learning algorithms," which play a crucial role in enabling these adjustments. The section also outlines the relationship between data analysis and decision-making processes within the adaptive framework, emphasizing how continuous monitoring and feedback loops are essential for maintaining optimal performance. While no specific tables are mentioned, the discussion underscores the importance of integrating various technical components to create an effective adaptive platform.

nodes.title: 2.1 Design and Target Configuration Models
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document explains the distinction between design models and target-configuration models in AUTOSAR. It introduces key terms such as "target-configuration," which encompasses configurations specific to a particular target, like ECU Configuration (EcuC) for an EcuInstance on the classic platform or Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) for machines on the adaptive platform. The section notes that while the distinction between these models is generally clear, some model elements can have characteristics of both design and configuration, making the boundary sometimes unclear. It also clarifies that "deployment" was previously used to describe aspects of target-configuration but has been replaced with more precise terminology to avoid confusion.

nodes.title: 2.1.1 Design Model
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document discusses the criteria for identifying design-related model elements in system architecture. It defines these elements as those typically created by System Designers and that contribute to the overall system's external behavior, rather than just a single compute node. Key concepts include the inability to modify such elements during target-configuration without breaking the system, and their role in exchanging information between different roles like System Designer and Integrator or Application Developer and System Designer. The section emphasizes the importance of these design-related models in ensuring consistent and reliable system operation across various roles involved in the development process.

nodes.title: 2.1.2 Target Configuration Models
nodes.summary: This section discusses the target-configuration models in the context of adaptive platforms, focusing on how these models are created and their relationship to design-level models. Key concepts include model elements that are typically local to a compute node and are often generated during integration. The summary highlights that some target-configuration elements maintain direct relations with design-level elements, while others are derived from them, as seen in the AUTOSAR classic Platform's ECU Configuration model (EcuC). Notably, the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) assumes parts of the design model will be available on the target in a machine-specific format rather than a standalone ARXML configuration.

nodes.title: 2.2 Overview
nodes.summary: This section provides an overview of the configuration process for the AUTOSAR adaptive Platform, highlighting its similarities and differences compared to the classic platform. It introduces key concepts such as the M2 and M1 models, which represent the design and configuration models respectively, and explains their relationship through serialization processes. The summary outlines that the M2 model defines the structure used by the M1 model, ensuring validation against an AUTOSAR XML schema when serialized to ARXML files. Additionally, it describes how the "definition side" (M2) constrains the "value side" (M1), emphasizing the hierarchical and interdependent nature of these models in the configuration process.

nodes.title: 2.3 Benefits of using an M1 model-based configuration approach
nodes.summary: This section discusses the advantages of using an M1 model-based configuration approach for the AUTOSAR adaptive Platform compared to a more detailed M2 level implementation. Key concepts include simpler model structures that facilitate easier translation into vendor-specific configurations, and the ability to define default values, use function-cluster-specific terminology, and specify multiplicities beyond standard ranges. The approach also enables rapid adaptation to changing requirements without waiting for schema changes or compromising backwards compatibility at the schema level. Additionally, it highlights a clear distinction between design and configuration workflows.

nodes.title: 2.4 Modeling Strategies for deriving M1 Model Content
nodes.summary: This section discusses the modeling strategies for deriving M1 model content in the context of configuring the AUTOSAR adaptive platform. It highlights that configuration models often need to integrate information from design models to provide a comprehensive picture. The text suggests that various approaches can be taken to combine design model elements with configuration-level information, indicating flexibility in implementation. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, the section emphasizes the interdependence between design and configuration models as crucial for effective platform setup.

nodes.title: 2.4.1 Only Design exists
nodes.summary: This section discusses scenarios where the configuration of a functional cluster can be entirely derived from a design model, eliminating the need for an initial configuration model. It introduces the concept that in certain rare cases, the design model alone suffices to define all necessary configurations. The summary also mentions Figure 2.4, which visually represents this modeling strategy, highlighting the relationship between the design and configuration models. While no specific tables are referenced, this section emphasizes the flexibility and potential simplification of the platform's machine configuration process by leveraging a single design model.

nodes.title: 2.4.2 Fully derive Design Model to Configuration Model
nodes.summary: This section discusses a modeling strategy aimed at fully deriving all relevant information from the design model into the configuration model. The key concept here is that once this derivation process is complete, the design model becomes redundant and can be deleted. This approach mirrors the AUTOSAR classic Platform's method for configuring the Com stack but has faced criticism due to potential information duplication, leading to a complex and large configuration model. While effective, this strategy requires careful management to avoid unnecessary complexity.

nodes.title: 2.4.3 Partially derive Design Model to Configuration Model
nodes.summary: This section discusses an alternative approach to deriving a configuration model from a design model in the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration system. It introduces the concept of partially deriving the configuration model and maintaining flat references or instanceRefs back to the design model, depending on the complexity of the derived models. The strategy avoids using the AUTOSAR type-prototype pattern for parts requiring flat references. Key components include the use of instanceRefs in the meta-model where the type-prototype pattern was previously applied, and visualizations (Figures 2.6 and 2.7) illustrate these strategies.

nodes.title: 2.4.4 Only Configuration exists
nodes.summary: This section discusses the modeling strategy where no design model exists at higher levels; instead, all configuration is performed on the M1 level. This approach is considered unique and not typical in standard practices. The summary includes a visualization (Figure 2.8) that illustrates this specific modeling scenario. While there are no tables provided in the given content, it emphasizes the exclusive focus on configuration at the lowest model level without any higher-level design models influencing or being present.

nodes.title: 2.5 Vendor Specific Functional Cluster Definition
nodes.summary: This section discusses the definition and usage of Vendor-Specific Functional Cluster Definitions within the context of AUTOSAR's standardized framework. It introduces key terms such as `STANDARDIZED_CLUSTER_DEFINITION` and `VENDOR_SPECIFIC_CLUSTER_DEFINITION`, explaining how vendors can extend the standardized definitions to include their own specific elements while maintaining consistency with AUTOSAR standards. The section also outlines constraints on the use of these definitions, specifically that only predefined values for the `category` attribute are allowed in a draft status, and custom values are not currently supported. This approach facilitates tool development and future standardization efforts by allowing vendors to add their own extensions while adhering to AUTOSAR's core standards.

structure.title: 3 Overview
structure.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document provides an overview of the configuration elements and their modeling strategy. It introduces two key components: the "definition side," which outlines the structure for specific parts of the target-configuration, such as a functional cluster; and the "value side," which assigns values to these configuration items based on the defined structure. The section emphasizes that these two parts work in tandem to create a comprehensive machine configuration model. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, the focus is on understanding how the configuration elements are structured and populated to meet specific requirements.

nodes.title: 3.1 Abstract Structure
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document outlines the abstract structure defining the relationship between the "value side" and the "definition side" in machine configuration. Key concepts include `ApmcAbstractDefinition` and `ApmcAbstractValue`, which represent the "definition" and "value" sides, respectively. The section introduces constraints such as the existence of a definition for an `ApmcAbstractValue`. It also specifies that multiple `ApmcAbstractValue` instances can reference the same `ApmcAbstractDefinition`. Tables 3.1 and 3.2 detail the attributes and inheritance relationships of these classes, with `definition` in `ApmcAbstractValue` serving as a critical reference linking the two sides.

nodes.title: 3.2 Definition Side
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on defining key elements and their relationships in the configuration process for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces two primary classes: `ApmcDefinitionCollection` and `ApmcFunctionalClusterDef`. The `ApmcDefinitionCollection` acts as a container that aggregates multiple `ApmcFunctionalClusterDefs`, serving as an entry point for target-configuration on the "definition" side. Each `ApmcFunctionalClusterDef` represents a single functional cluster configuration, containing attributes such as `container`, which is aggregated directly by the functional cluster definition and marked with specific stereotypes like `atpSplitable`. The tables provide detailed information about these classes, including their base classes, attributes, and tags that define their usage and constraints within the platform.

nodes.title: 3.2.1 Definition Elements
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on defining elements for the target configuration in the context of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the `ApmcDefinitionElement` abstract class, which provides essential attributes such as `elementCreation`, `lowerMultiplicity`, and `upperMultiplicity`. These attributes help define how model elements are created and their multiplicity within the configuration. The document also introduces the `ApmcConfigurationElementDef` abstract class, which includes the `origin` attribute to distinguish between standardized AUTOSAR elements and vendor-specific configurations. Importantly, it describes how ordered collections can be managed through explicit index values for elements in a collection, ensuring that their order is defined semantically rather than syntactically.

nodes.title: 3.2.2 Grouping of Configuration Elements
nodes.summary: This section discusses the grouping of configuration elements within a container in the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration approach. It introduces key classes such as `ApmcContainerDef`, `ApmcContainerElementDef`, and `ApmcParamConfContainerDef` that facilitate simple grouping, nested structures, and reference creation between containers at different nesting levels. The `ApmcParamConfContainerDef` class is central to this mechanism, defining the structure of parameter and reference definitions within a container, with attributes like `container` and `element` for aggregating sub-containers and elements respectively. These classes enable complex hierarchical configurations while supporting candidate status in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: 3.2.3 Emulation of Inheritance
nodes.summary: This section discusses the emulation of inheritance in the target-configuration using an ApmcChoiceContainerDef. The key concept here is that this meta-class allows for specifying choice semantics at the container level, effectively mimicking inheritance in the M2 model through the M1 model. Specifically, it introduces the `ApmcChoiceContainerDef` class and its attributes, such as the `choice` collection, which represents the content of the enclosing choice container. The section also visualizes this relationship using Figure 3.3, illustrating how choices on the definition side in the target-configuration map to sub-class relationships in the M2 model. Relevant technical details from Table 3.11 highlight that `ApmcChoiceContainerDef` is a candidate for use in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform and provides important metadata like stereotypes and tags for further customization and identification.

nodes.title: 3.2.4 Parameter
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on defining and managing configuration parameters within the context of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the abstract class `ApmcParameterDef`, which serves as a base for various parameter definitions, such as boolean, string, or integer types. These parameters can only be defined within an `ApmcParamConfContainerDef` and are described by subclasses like `ApmcBooleanParamDef` and `ApmcStringParamDef`. The section also outlines the relationship between these parameters and their container, emphasizing that they are integral to configuring specific aspects of the platform's target configuration. Table 3.12 provides a detailed breakdown of the abstract class `ApmcParameterDef`, including its attributes and relationships with other classes.

nodes.title: 3.2.5 Reference
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document discusses the concept and implementation of references within the target-configuration framework of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the abstract meta-class `ApmcReferenceDef`, which serves as a foundational class for various reference definitions, such as foreign references and instance references. The section explains that references can be used to establish semantic links between different parts of the configuration or associate specific elements with components from the design model. Although no attributes are listed in the provided table, `ApmcReferenceDef` is aggregated by `ApmcParamConfContainerDef.element`, indicating its role in parameter configuration containers. The class hierarchy includes several subclasses that further specify the nature of these references, and the section references additional details for deeper understanding in a subsequent section (4.1.2).

nodes.title: 3.3 Value Side
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on the value side of the target configuration, detailing how actual configuration values are provided and must reference corresponding definitions for correct interpretation and validation. Key concepts include `ApmcFunctionalClusterValue`, `ApmcContainerValue`, and `ApmcContainerElementValue`, each required to have a defined reference to their respective configurations on the definition side. The document also introduces `ApmcValueCollection` as a collector class that aggregates multiple `ApmcFunctionalClusterValues`. Importantly, constraints ensure that references from the value side to the definition side must exist for all relevant configuration elements. Technical details from tables specify attributes and relationships, such as the aggregation of containers within functional clusters and the reference to corresponding definitions, highlighting the structured nature of these configurations in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.

nodes.title: 3.3.1 Grouping of Configuration Values
nodes.summary: This section discusses the grouping and nested structure of configuration values in the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) system. It introduces key classes such as `ApmcContainerValue` and `ApmcContainerElementValue`, which are used to represent containers and elements on the "value" side of the target-configuration. The `ApmcContainerValue` class can aggregate itself, containing multiple `ApmcContainerElementValue` instances. Each element is defined by a reference (`definition`) that links back to its corresponding configuration in the "definition" side. Table 3.16 and Table 3.17 provide detailed attributes and relationships for these classes, emphasizing their roles within the APMC framework.

nodes.title: 3.3.2 Parameter
nodes.summary: This section discusses the `ApmcParameterValue` class within the context of the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration. It serves as an abstract base class for configuration parameters that can take various forms, such as numerical values, textual data, or restricted text. The class is part of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform and inherits from multiple classes including `ARObject`, highlighting its role in defining parameter values across different configurations. Subclasses like `ApmcAbstractRestrictedTextualParamValue`, `ApmcNumericalParamValue`, and `ApmcTextualParamValue` provide concrete implementations for these abstract parameters, while the class is aggregated by `ApmcContainerValue.element`. The table outlines key attributes of this class, though no specific details are provided in the summary.

nodes.title: 3.3.3 Reference
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document discusses the `ApmcReferenceValue` abstract base class, which represents references on the "value side" of the adaptive platform. Key concepts include its role in clarifying the concrete nature of references through subclasses like `ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceValue`, `ApmcAbstractReferenceValue`, and `ApmcContainerReferenceValue`. The class is specifically used within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, inheriting from multiple base classes such as `ARObject` and `Identifiable MultilanguageReferrable`. Additionally, Table 3.19 outlines the structure of `ApmcReferenceValue`, though it does not provide specific attribute details in this summary.

structure.title: 4 Details
structure.summary: This section of "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" delves into the intricate details of configuring various elements within an adaptive platform. It introduces key concepts such as configuration parameters and their interdependencies, which are crucial for optimizing machine performance. The summary highlights important relationships between different components and outlines constraints that must be considered during setup. Additionally, it includes relevant technical details from accompanying tables, providing a comprehensive guide for users to understand and implement the configurations effectively.

nodes.title: 4.1 Definition Side
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on detailing configuration elements found on the "definition side," which is crucial for understanding how machine configurations are specified and managed. Key concepts include specific parameters and attributes that define machine behavior, such as performance settings, resource allocation policies, and operational constraints. The section also outlines important relationships between these definitions and their impact on system performance and resource utilization. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in the provided content, it is implied that technical details about configuration parameters and their interactions would be presented, likely with examples or constraints to guide proper configuration practices.

nodes.title: 4.1.1 Parameter
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on detailing the parameter definitions used in the modeling process. It introduces key elements through an overview depicted in Figure 4.1, which provides a comprehensive view of how manifest parameters are defined. The summary highlights that these parameters are crucial for configuring the adaptive platform, and their detailed definition is essential for ensuring proper machine configuration. While no specific tables are mentioned, the section likely outlines important relationships between different parameter types and constraints that must be adhered to during the modeling process.

nodes.title: 4.1.1.1 Enumeration Parameter
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on defining and managing enumeration-style parameters within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces two key meta-classes: `ApmcEnumerationParamDef` for modeling enumerations, and `ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef` for specifying the values that these enumerations can take. The section also outlines how to define default values for such parameters, which are automatically assigned when a parameter is instantiated without a specific value. Relationships between classes are established through aggregation, with `ApmcEnumerationParamDef` containing multiple `ApmcEnumerationLiteralDef` instances via the `literal` role. Constraints and statuses of these meta-classes are noted, emphasizing their use within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform context.

nodes.title: 4.1.1.2 Boolean Parameter
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on the `ApmcBooleanParamDef` meta-class, which is used to define boolean configuration parameters. Key components include the relationship between `ApmcBooleanParamDef` and `Apmc-NumericalParamValue`, as well as the ability to set a default value for these parameters according to specific regulations. The document also outlines that this class is part of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, with detailed attributes such as `defaultValue` that are crucial for defining how boolean parameters behave when not explicitly assigned a value. Table 4.3 provides further technical details on the structure and usage of `ApmcBooleanParamDef`.

nodes.title: 4.1.1.3 Integer Parameter
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on the `ApmcIntegerParamDef` meta-class, which is used to define integer configuration parameters in the context of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the relationship between `ApmcIntegerParamDef` and its corresponding value class `Apmc-NumericalParamValue`, as well as attributes such as `defaultValue`, `min`, and `max` that allow for setting default values and defining allowed ranges. The document also outlines how this meta-class is aggregated within a configuration container (`ApmcParamConfContainerDef.element`) and provides relevant technical details through Table 4.4, which lists the attributes of `ApmcIntegerParamDef` along with their types, multiplicities, and notes.

nodes.title: 4.1.1.4 Float Parameter
nodes.summary: This section discusses the `ApmcFloatParamDef` meta-class in the context of the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration, focusing on its role in defining floating-point configuration parameters. Key concepts include the default value and allowed range (min and max) for these parameters. The class is aggregated by `ApmcParamConfContainerDef.element`, indicating how it fits into a larger configuration structure. Importantly, constraints are defined through attributes such as `defaultValue`, `max`, and `min`, ensuring that any instantiated parameter adheres to specified limits.

nodes.title: 4.1.1.5 Unrestricted String Parameter
nodes.summary: This section describes the `ApmcStringParamDef` meta-class in the context of the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces key concepts such as the definition of a string parameter with unrestricted content and its relationship to other classes like `ApmcTextualParamValue`. The section also details that `ApmcStringParamDef` can have a default value defined, which is applicable if no specific value is assigned during instantiation. Table 4.6 outlines the attributes of this class, including the `defaultValue`, which is represented as a `VerbatimString`.

nodes.title: 4.1.1.6 Restricted String Parameter
nodes.summary: This section discusses the implementation of restricted string parameters in the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) for various use cases. It introduces key meta-classes such as `ApmcAbstractRestrictedStringParamDef`, which serves as an abstract base class for defining string parameters with specific content restrictions, and its subclasses like `ApmcIpV4AddressParamDef` and `ApmcRevisionLabelParamDef`. These classes enforce predefined patterns to ensure that the string values conform to certain formats (e.g., IP addresses, revision labels). The section also details attributes such as `defaultValue`, which implements default values for these restricted parameters. Importantly, it explains how these restrictions are uniformly applied and validated at the XML level through specific regular expressions defined in the meta-classes.

nodes.title: 4.1.2 Reference
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on detailing how references are modeled in the system. It introduces three key types of references: container references, foreign references (also known as element references from the M2 model), and instance references to elements of the M2 model (referred to as `instanceRef`). These reference types are crucial for defining relationships within the platform's configuration manifest. The section also mentions that a detailed overview of these reference definitions is provided in Figure 4.3, which likely illustrates how each type of reference fits into the overall system architecture and interacts with other components.

nodes.title: 4.1.2.1 Reference to Container
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on the semantics and usage of container references within the M1 model. It introduces key concepts such as `ApmcContainerReferenceDef` for referencing a single container and `ApmcChoiceContainerReferenceDef` for referencing multiple containers, where only one can be selected at any given time. The section outlines that these meta-classes are used independently of the target's definition scope within the same functional cluster. Importantly, it specifies that the `destination` attribute in both classes should contain a fully-qualified short name of the reference target and details the attributes and relationships involved, such as `isFuncClusterEndpointRef`.

nodes.title: 4.1.2.2 Foreign Reference
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on the modeling and semantics of foreign references within the context of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the abstract base class `ApmcAbstractForeignReferenceDef`, which defines how to establish a flat reference (as opposed to an instance reference) to model elements outside the target configuration, specifically in the design model. The document introduces three specific subclasses: `ApmcUriForeignReferenceDef` and `ApmcUpstreamDocForeignReferenceDef`, each with distinct roles—latter two allowing references that may not exist within the same model scope or referencing the element from which a container was derived by derivation, respectively. Importantly, these classes are only used in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform and are marked as candidate status, indicating they are still under consideration for final implementation.

nodes.title: 4.1.2.3 Instance Reference
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on instance references in the context of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces key concepts such as `ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceDef`, which defines structured references to model elements, and its subclasses like `ApmcInstanceReferenceDef` and `ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef`. The section details attributes like `destinationContext` and `destinationType`, which restrict the possible reference targets. Additionally, it describes how these references can be used in both design and configuration domains, with constraints on their existence within model scopes. The document also outlines another meta-class, `ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceDef`, which serves as a metadata element to track the derivation of containers from design elements.

nodes.title: 4.2 Value Side
nodes.summary: This section of "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" focuses on the detailed configuration elements related to the "value side," which are crucial for optimizing resource allocation and performance. Key concepts include specific parameters such as value thresholds, priority levels, and resource quotas that define how resources are managed based on their assigned values. The section also describes important relationships between these configurations and the overall system architecture, emphasizing constraints like maximum capacity limits to ensure balanced resource distribution. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, the content likely includes technical details about how these configurations interact with the platform's adaptive mechanisms to dynamically adjust resource allocation in response to varying workload demands.

nodes.title: 4.2.1 Parameter
nodes.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on defining and explaining key attributes related to indexable values within collections. It introduces the abstract meta-class `ApmcIndexableValue`, which is crucial for modeling elements that carry an index in the target-configuration, and specifies its subclasses such as `ApmcContainerElementValue` and `ApmcContainerValue`. The document also clarifies that while individual index attributes within an ordered collection may have gaps, their ordering is based on numerical sorting rather than sequential usage. Additionally, it provides detailed definitions for attributes like `index`, which represents the logical position of elements in a collection with specific semantics, as documented in Table 4.30 and Table 4.31.

nodes.title: 4.2.1.2 Unrestricted Textual Parameter Value
nodes.summary: This section discusses the handling of textual parameters in an adaptive platform machine configuration (Apmc) system. It introduces key concepts such as `ApmcEnumerationParamDef` and `ApmcStringParamDef`, emphasizing that enumeration-style parameters can only accept predefined values, while string parameters allow for arbitrary input. The section outlines a constraint where using `ApmcEnumerationParamDef` is not suitable for accepting non-predefined textual inputs, advocating instead the use of `ApmcStringParamDef`. Table 4.32, "ApmcTextualParamValue," likely details the structure and characteristics of these textual parameter values, providing a comprehensive reference for their implementation and usage within the system.

nodes.title: 4.2.1.3 Restricted Textual Parameter Value
nodes.summary: This section discusses the implementation and usage of restricted textual parameter values within the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) framework. It introduces key sub-classes such as `ApmcAbstractRestrictedTextualParamValue`, which are used to define parameters with specific textual constraints, and their corresponding subclasses like `ApmcRevisionLabelParamValue` and `ApmcIpV4AddressParamValue`. The section emphasizes that each subclass must reference the appropriate definition class, ensuring type consistency. Tables 4.33 through 4.39 detail these classes' attributes and relationships, highlighting that they are specifically tailored for use in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform to enforce structural restrictions on parameter values.

nodes.title: 4.2.2 Reference
nodes.summary: This section of the document focuses on various reference meta-classes used in the Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration (APMC) for establishing references within and between different model elements. Key concepts include `ApmcContainerReferenceValue`, which allows referencing another container, and its subclasses like `ApmcForeignReferenceValue` and `ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue`, which provide flat references to external design model elements. The section also introduces `ApmcAbstractInstanceReferenceValue` and its sub-classes for structured references, such as `ApmcInstanceReferenceValue` and `ApmcUriInstanceReferenceValue`. These references are crucial for linking configuration models with the design models in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Relationships between these meta-classes and their attributes are detailed, particularly through tables that specify attribute types, multiplicities, and kinds, ensuring clear definitions of how each reference operates within the platform's architecture.

structure.title: 5 Modeling Use Cases
structure.summary: This section of the "Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration" document focuses on specific modeling use cases where elements from the document are combined to achieve predefined higher-level semantics according to the AUTOSAR standard. Key concepts include the integration and application of various model components in practical scenarios, highlighting how these elements can be used to define complex system behaviors. The section likely describes important relationships between different model elements and any constraints or processes that must be followed when combining them. While specific technical details are not provided here, relevant information may be found in accompanying tables detailing the interactions and configurations of these models.

nodes.title: 5.1 Functional Cluster interacts with Functional Cluster
nodes.summary: This section discusses the interaction between functional clusters in an adaptive platform machine configuration. It introduces key terms such as "FunctionalClusterEndpointSpecifier" and outlines a specific modeling approach for interactions where one cluster (the client) requests services from another cluster (the server). The summary highlights that this interaction is governed by constraints defined in [AP_TPS_APMC_01039] and [constr_10644], which detail how endpoints must match based on certain attributes. Relevant technical details include the use of `ApmcContainerReferenceDef` for client-side configuration and `ApmcParamConfContainerDef` for server-side configuration, ensuring that interactions are properly established between functional clusters.
